properties.name: Jeen Mata Temple, Rewasa
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Jeen Mata — daughter of Chauhan king Ghangh, worshipped as an incarnation of Durga.
The temple at Rewasa (Sikar) dates to the 8th c.; Aurangzeb reportedly halted his temple-destruction campaign after being visited in a dream by the goddess.
Twin fairs at Chaitra and Ashwin Navratri draw ~500,000 pilgrims each — Jeen Mata is the kul devi of the Chauhan clans.
The Sam Gau Mata Mandir at Rewasa is India's fourth Gau Mata Temple.

properties.name: Shila Mata Temple, Amber Fort
properties.deity: Shila Mata (Kul Devi of Kachhawaha Rajputs)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Shila Mata (also Shila Devi) — the tutelary Kul Devi of the Kachhawaha Jaipur royal house.
The idol was brought from Jessore (in modern-day Bangladesh) by Raja Man Singh I after his campaign in Bengal (1604).
Housed in the Ganesh Pol courtyard of Amber Fort; the shrine's animal sacrifice was banned in 1975.
One of the few Rajasthan temples where a non-Rajasthani stone idol (Bengali black basalt) is worshipped as a Kul Devi.

properties.name: Sacchiyay Mata Temple, Osian
properties.deity: Sacchiyay Mata (Kul Devi of Oswal Jains)
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Sacchiyay Mata — the Kul Devi of the Oswal Jain merchant community (Rajasthan's largest Jain sub-caste).
Osian temple (built 8-9th c. under Pratiharas) is the pilgrimage centre — Rajasthan's oldest surviving Jain sanctuary.
The Osian Jain temple cluster (12+ temples) is India's most-complete surviving early-medieval Pratihara Jain-Hindu architecture.
The town of Osian is called 'Khajuraho of Rajasthan' for its concentration of 8-9th-c. sculpture.

properties.name: Rani Sati Temple, Jhunjhunu
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Rani Sati of Dadhich family — worshipped by the Marwari-Agrawal-Maheshwari trading communities.
The Jhunjhunu temple (rebuilt 1980s) is the world's largest Sati Mata temple.
Attracts 3+ million pilgrims annually from Marwari diaspora across India and abroad.
The Rani Sati fair (Bhadrapada Amavasya) is one of Rajasthan's largest merchant-community pilgrimages.

properties.name: Sundha Mata Temple, Jalore
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Sundha Mata — worshipped as an aspect of Chamunda; tutelary goddess of the Chauhan-Devra branch.
Temple sits at 1220 m on Sundha Hill, western Aravalli — Rajasthan's first commercial ropeway to a hill shrine (2006).
The Sundha Hill inscription (1262 CE) records the Chauhan-Devra pledge of protection for the shrine.
The Chandan Mata annexe holds one of Rajasthan's oldest surviving Chandan wood carvings.

properties.name: Chamunda Mata Temple, Mehrangarh
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Chamunda Mata — tutelary Kul Devi of the Jodhpur Rathore dynasty.
Idol was brought to Jodhpur by Rao Jodha from Mandore in 1459 when Mehrangarh was founded.
The 2008 stampede here — during Navratri — killed 249 devotees, one of India's worst temple stampedes.
The shrine sits at the highest point of Mehrangarh Fort; visible from all across Jodhpur city.

properties.name: Aadhar Devi Temple, Mount Abu
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + Rajasthan folk records

facts: Aadhar Devi (Arbuda Devi) — the goddess of Mount Abu; her name gave the mountain its ancient title 'Arbuda-giri'.
The idol is enshrined in a natural cave — reached via 365 stone steps.
One of Rajasthan's earliest continuously-worshipped Shakti Peethas — mentioned in the Skanda Purana.
During Navratri, the shrine draws pilgrims from the Bhil, Garasia and Rabari communities of the Aravalli.
